completeness.rationale: All seven expected extracts are present: 2-4 hour delay, started ~1 week ago, worse 9-10am/better afternoon, related to marketing digest batch, queue congestion hypothesis, shared pipeline between transactional and bulk, and prior 1-2 minute delivery time. The summary also adds useful information not explicitly in the issue (in-app notifications unaffected, ~200 org size, detailed reproduction steps, proposed test case).

accuracy.rationale: The agent correctly follows an acceptable diagnostic path: identifying the digest email feature as the cause and suspecting queue congestion on a shared pipeline. The recommended fix even explicitly mentions priority queues. The main inaccuracy is scale: the agent assumed ~200 digest emails (one per user) when the actual volume is ~10,000, which affects the severity estimate. The agent did not identify the priority inversion mechanism specifically, but that is not required by the acceptable paths.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked two well-chosen rounds of questions before resolving, covering timeline (what changed), timing correlation, org scale, and email-vs-in-app isolation. Correctly flagged remaining gaps in the summary. One obvious question was skipped: explicitly asking how many digest emails go out (not just how many users), which would have revealed the 10K volume and refined the hypothesis. Otherwise coverage was strong.

economy.rationale: Only two question turns were used, both high-value. The second turn efficiently combined two sub-questions (timing correlation and in-app isolation) without feeling bloated. No redundant or low-value questions.

question_quality.rationale: The first question (what changed ~1 week ago?) was a well-framed open probe that surfaced the digest feature — information the reporter might not have volunteered. The second question's in-app notification check was particularly insightful diagnostic reasoning: it successfully isolated the problem to email delivery specifically, ruling out task assignment pipeline issues. Both questions demonstrated genuine causal reasoning rather than generic checklists.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary gives a developer an immediate starting point: check whether digest and transactional emails share a queue, apply priority separation, check rate limiting, and consider staggering. Reproduction steps are specific (9am vs 3-4pm assignment comparison, queue depth logging). The proposed test case is directly implementable. Even with the scale underestimate, a developer could begin investigating and likely arrive at the correct fix.

notable_strengths: The in-app notification isolation question was a genuinely insightful diagnostic move that cleanly scoped the problem to email delivery and ruled out broader pipeline issues
The first open-ended question ('what changed around that time?') successfully surfaced the digest feature without leading the reporter
The recommended fix independently arrives at priority queue separation, closely matching the actual fix, despite not knowing the internal queue architecture
notable_weaknesses: The agent assumed one digest email per user (~200 total) rather than asking about email volume explicitly; the actual 10K volume is what creates the multi-hour backlog, and the scale matters for the fix
Did not confirm the exact date the digest feature was enabled relative to when delays began, leaving a minor evidentiary gap noted only as an information_gap
most_insightful_question: Does TaskFlow show you in-app notifications (like a bell icon or notification panel) when tasks are assigned? If so, do those appear on time, or are they delayed too?
missed_opportunities: How many digest emails go out each morning — is it one per user or a larger batch? (Would have revealed the 10K volume)
Have you confirmed with other teammates that they're also experiencing the same email delays, or is it just you? (Would have confirmed systemic scope)
Do you know approximately when the digest feature was first enabled — was it exactly one week ago or thereabouts? (Would have tightened the timeline correlation)
